segments.inferno-29-g1: This clause uses an archaic imperfect of avere, 'avean' (modern Italian 'avevano') as the auxiliary meaning 'had' and a past participle construction: 'La molta gente e le diverse piaghe avean le luci mie sì inebrïate' = 'So many people and the various wounds had my lights so intoxicated.' Word order places the two plural subjects before the verb; 'le luci mie' is a poetic way to say 'my sight/eyes' (lit. 'my lights'). 'Sì' is an adverb meaning 'so' and modifies 'inebrïate' (figurative 'intoxicated').
segments.inferno-29-g2: 'che' introduces the consequence clause 'that ...'; 'de lo stare a piangere' is an archaic way to express a gerundive/nominal action ('of/for the remaining to weep'), where 'de' links to the verbal noun phrase. 'Stare a piangere' is a periphrastic expression meaning 'to be/stand doing X' (here 'to remain weeping'). 'Eran vaghe' uses the imperfect 'eran' ('were') plus 'vaghe' meaning 'desirous' or 'longing for' — so the eyes were eager for the continued act of weeping.
segments.inferno-29-g3: Simple reporting clause: 'Ma' ('but') contrasts with the previous mood; 'Virgilio mi disse' is past simple/perfect reported speech (Dante uses 'disse' for 'said') and 'mi' is the indirect object 'to me'.
segments.inferno-29-g4: This is Virgil's abrupt interrogative: 'Che pur guate?' 'Che' here introduces a question ('what/why'); 'pur' = 'still' or 'even now'; 'guate' is an archaic/poetic form related to watching/guarding and here functions as the object of the question (roughly 'what are you still looking at?'). The line is elliptical and idiomatic rather than a full modern sentence.
segments.inferno-29-g5: 'Perché' introduces the question 'why'; 'la vista tua' is an inverted possessive ('your sight' with the article placed before 'vista' as in Italian). 'Pur' again means 'still' or 'even now'. The reflexive 'si' with the verb 'soffolge' (present indicative) means 'is overcome/seized' — so the phrase says your sight is still overcome/sank 'là giù' (there below) 'tra l'ombre triste smozzicate' ('among the sad, truncated shades'). Word order places the place adverbials after the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.inferno-29-g6: 'Tu non hai fatto sì a l’altre bolge' uses the perfect auxiliary 'hai' + past participle 'fatto' to mean 'you have done' or more loosely 'you did'; 'sì' = 'so' and the prepositional phrase 'a l’altre bolge' ('to/in the other bolges') indicates location/context — i.e., 'you did not act this way in the other bolges.'
segments.inferno-29-g7: Imperative or hortatory 'pensa,' (think/consider) addressed to the pilgrim — a direct command telling him to reckon or reflect on the following fact.
segments.inferno-29-g8: A conditional 'se' ('if') clause with explicit subject 'tu' and an archaic infinitive 'annoverar' (modern 'annoverare'/'annoverare' or 'annoverare') meaning 'to number/consider among.' 'Le' refers back to 'bolge' (feminine plural). 'Credi' is second-person singular present 'you believe' (you suppose). Together: 'if you suppose you can count them.'
segments.inferno-29-g9: 'che' introduces the content clause 'that ...'; 'miglia ventidue' = 'twenty-two miles' (note the inverted order: number after the noun), 'la valle volge' uses the present 'volge' (from volgere) meaning 'turns' or 'winds' — i.e., 'the valley winds/turns for twenty-two miles.' The verb is indicative and gives a factual detail that supports Virgil's warning.
segments.inferno-29-g85: 'Latin' names the people (Latins). 'siam' is an archaic present form of 'siamo' (first-person plural of essere — 'we are'), and 'noi' is the explicit subject pronoun used for emphasis after the verb.
segments.inferno-29-g86: 'che' is a relative pronoun linking back to 'noi' ('whom'); 'tu vedi' is present indicative 'you see'; 'sì' is an adverb meaning 'so/so very' modifying 'guasti'; 'guasti' is a past-participle/adjectival use meaning 'ruined' or 'damaged'; 'qui ambedue' places the location and emphasizes 'both (of us) here' (word order is emphatic).
segments.inferno-29-g87: 'rispuose' is the older past tense form (passato remoto) of 'rispondere' — 'answered'; 'l’ un' = 'l' + 'un' = 'one (of them)' (the article + numeral used to single out one speaker); 'piangendo' is a present participle functioning adverbially — 'weeping' (describes how he answered).
segments.inferno-29-g88: 'ma' is a contrasting conjunction 'but'; 'tu chi se’' = 'who are you' where 'se’' is an archaic form of 'sei' (second-person singular of essere); 'che' introduces a relative clause modifying 'tu' ('who are you who...'); 'di noi' is the prepositional object 'about/of us' governed by 'dimandasti'; 'dimandasti' is passato remoto, second-person singular 'you asked'.
segments.inferno-29-g89: Punctuation: a full stop marking the end of the preceding reported speech and the pause before the next speaker's reply.
segments.inferno-29-g90: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking to the prior action; 'l' duca' is the article + noun 'the duke/guide' (Virgil as guide); 'disse' is passato remoto third-person singular of 'dire' — 'said' — introducing his speech.
segments.inferno-29-g91: 'I’ son' = 'I am' where 'I’' is the clitic/apostrophized 'io' and 'son' is the archaic present of 'essere' (sono). 'un che discendo' = 'one who descends' (relative clause with 'che' + present 'discendo'). 'con questo vivo' means 'with this living one/man' (vivo used as 'living person'), and 'giù di balzo in balzo' is an idiomatic, adverbial phrase 'down from leap to leap' (repetition 'di...in...' marks the successive steps or bounds).
segments.inferno-29-g92: 'e di mostrar' — the 'di' introduces the infinitive 'mostrar' (older form of 'mostrare') as the complement of 'intendo'; 'intendo' is first-person singular present 'I intend'. 'lo 'nferno' (the apostrophe marks the elision) is the direct object 'Hell', and 'a lui' is the indirect object 'to him' (i.e., I intend to show Hell to him).
segments.inferno-29-g93: Allor is a temporal adverb meaning "then/thereupon." Si ruppe is ruppe (passato remoto, 3rd singular) with the reflexive si, giving an intransitive/reflexive sense "broke/was broken." Lo comun rincalzo is the noun phrase serving as the thing that broke: lo (definite article), comun (adjective "common" placed before the noun in poetic order), and rincalzo (noun "support/reinforcement").
segments.inferno-29-g94: E introduces the second clause ("and"). Tremando is a present participle/gerund (adverbial) meaning "trembling," describing a simultaneous action. Ciascuno is the subject "each one/everyone," and a me places the direction toward the speaker; si volse is volse (passato remoto, 3rd singular of volgere) with the reflexive si, "turned (himself)" or "turned toward." Con + altri introduces accompaniment "with others," che is a relative pronoun "who/which" referring to "altri," l' is the contracted direct object pronoun "it/them" attached to udiron, and udiron (passato remoto "they heard") plus the adverbial phrase di rimbalzo (di = "by," rimbalzo = "rebound/echo") gives "who heard it by rebound/echo."
segments.inferno-29-g130: Ma = but, a simple adversative particle starting the clause. Perché + sappi here introduces a purpose clause ('so that...'); perché is used as 'so that' and sappi is the present subjunctive (2nd singular) of sapere used for purpose/optative meaning 'that you may know' rather than a simple indicative. Chi ... seconda is a relative clause: chi = 'who', and seconda is 3rd-person singular present of secondare (to favor/support), so 'who thus supports you'. Contra i Sanesi = 'against the Sienese' with the preposition contra + definite article i marking the people targeted.
segments.inferno-29-g131: Aguzza is an imperative (2nd singular) of aguzzare = 'sharpen' (make keen), addressing the addressee; ver' is a contracted poetic form of verso meaning 'toward'. Me is the object 'me'; l' is the article and occhio is the noun 'eye' (literally 'sharpen your eye toward me' = 'look sharply at me').
segments.inferno-29-g132: Sì che introduces a consecutive/result clause here ('so that'); che = 'that'. La faccia mia = 'my face' with la + faccia + possessive mia. Ben modifies the verb that follows ('well/clearly'). Ti risponda uses risponda in the present subjunctive (3rd singular) after sì che to express result ('that my face may answer you' / 'may show itself to you'); risponda = 'answer' or 'respond'.
segments.inferno-29-g133: Sì here = 'thus' or 'so' linking back to the previous instruction. Vedrai is future 2nd singular 'you will see'. Ch'io son = 'that I am' with ch' = che (that), io subject pronoun for emphasis, and son an archaic short form of sono (1st singular present 'am'). L'ombra di Capocchio = 'the shade (ghost) of Capocchio' with ombra as 'shade/shadow/ghost' and Capocchio used as a name or nickname.
segments.inferno-29-g134: Che introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. Falsai is the first-person singular passato remoto of falsare = 'I falsified/counterfeited' (Dante speaks as the shade of Capocchio). Li metalli = 'the metals' i.e. coinage (li is the direct-object article). Con l'alchìmia = 'by/with alchemy', con + definite article + alchimia; alchìmia names the fraudulent art used.
segments.inferno-29-g135: E = and. Te is the tonic/accusative form 'you' (emphatic object). Dee is an archaic present singular of dovere (modern devi/dover) meaning 'you must' or 'you ought to' (here it is used impersonally: 'you must remember'). Ricordar is the infinitive 'remember' (used after dee/you must).
segments.inferno-29-g136: Se ben t'adocchio is a conditional clause meaning 'if I well behold/lay eyes on you': se = 'if'; ben = 'well'; t' = enclitic object 'you'; adocchio here is first-person present of adocchiare (poetic/archaic) meaning 'I perceive/see clearly' or 'I have in my sight'. The clause qualifies the speaker's ability to recognize the addressee.
segments.inferno-29-g137: Com' = contracted come 'as' or 'how'. Io fui is past tense (passato remoto) 'I was'. Di natura = 'by nature' or 'in disposition'. Buona scimia = 'a good scimia' where buona qualifies scimia; scimia literally 'ape' but in context is figurative 'mimic/imitator' — the phrase means 'how by nature I was a good ape' (i.e., naturally given to imitation).
segments.inferno-29-g10: Literal order: conjunction + adverb + subject + verb + prepositional phrase. E (and) links this line to the previous speech; già is an adverb 'already'; la luna is the subject; è is the present indicative of essere; sotto i nostri piedi is a spatial prepositional phrase meaning 'beneath our feet.'
segments.inferno-29-g11: Lo tempo (the time) is the grammatical subject; è poco = 'is little/short' states scarcity. Omai is an adverb meaning 'now' or 'by now'; che introduces a relative clause. N’ is the clitic ne (here 'of it' / 'of that') attached to è, so n’è concesso = 'has been granted of it' → 'has been granted to us.' Concesso is the past participle 'granted.'
segments.inferno-29-g12: E (and) links the clause; altro is a neuter 'other' meaning 'other things'; è da veder uses da + infinitive to express 'there is to be seen' / 'there remains to be seen' (impersonal construction). Che introduces the relative clause 'that'; tu non vedi is the restrictive clause 'that you do not see.'
segments.inferno-29-g13: Se introduces a contrary-to-fact conditional ('If...'); tu is the subject pronoun. Avessi here is the imperfect subjunctive (of avere), the protasis of a past counterfactual condition — the clause is elliptical (the rest of the conditional idea is completed later).
segments.inferno-29-g14: Rispuos' is a contracted poetic form of risposi/rispose 'I replied' (apostrophe marks an elision); the verb precedes the subject io (inverted word order common in poetry). Appresso is an adverb meaning 'shortly after' or 'soon after,' indicating temporal relation to the preceding speech.
segments.inferno-29-g15: Atteso is the past participle of attendere used here in the sense 'attending to / having regard to' (i.e., 'if you had paid attention to'); a la cagion = 'to the cause/reason.' Per introduces the relative clause per ch’io guardava = 'for which I was watching' (ch’ = che). Guardava is the imperfect of guardare, indicating an ongoing past action ('I was watching').
segments.inferno-29-g16: Forse = 'perhaps.' M’ is the clitic mi (me) as direct object. Avresti is the conditional auxiliary (second person singular), here used to form a conditional meaning 'you would [have]' or 'you would make/keep.' Ancor = 'still.' Lo star dimesso is a nominalized phrase: lo + infinitive star (state/being) + dimesso (downcast/abashed) — together meaning 'that downcast state' and functioning as a predicative object (i.e., 'you would still have me in that downcast state').
segments.inferno-29-g17: Parte here functions like an indefinite 'part' or 'some (of them)'; sen giva is the contracted se ne giva = 'went away/off' (se ne + giva), with giva the imperfect of andare 'was going/went.' The phrasing describes others moving on.
segments.inferno-29-g18: E (and) links clauses; io is the subject; retro is an adverb 'behind'/'after'; li is an old pronominal form referring to 'him' (object clitic); andava is the imperfect of andare, 'I went / I walked' — imperfect indicating contemporaneous past action ('I followed behind him').
segments.inferno-29-g19: Lo is the definite article; duca here denotes 'the leader/guide' (Virgil) rather than a secular duke — it's an appositive identifying whom the narrator followed.
segments.inferno-29-g20: Già 'already'; faccendo is a gerund (making/doing) used to indicate an action simultaneous with the main verb — 'already making the answer'; la risposta is the object 'the answer/response.' The gerund describes the master's ongoing action while the narrator followed.
segments.inferno-29-g21: E (and) links; soggiugnendo (variant of soggiungendo) is a gerund from soggiugnere meaning 'adding (as he spoke)' — it signals that what follows is an added remark by the master.
segments.inferno-29-g22: Dentro a quella cava = 'inside that hollow' (dentro + a forms a locative phrase). Dov’ (dove) introduces a relative clause 'where'; io is subject; tenea is the imperfect of tenere 'I held / I kept'; or = 'now/just now'; li occhi = 'the eyes' (article + noun); sì a posta = 'so fixed / purposely placed' (a posta = deliberately / in a set place). Together: 'where I had my eyes fixed just now.'
segments.inferno-29-g23: Credo = 'I believe.' Ch’ = che, introducing the subordinate clause. Un spirto del mio sangue = 'a spirit of my blood' (i.e., a kinsman or one of my bloodline). Pianga is the present subjunctive of piangere — after credo it presents the remark with a tone of conjecture or indirect statement ('I believe that ... laments'). La colpa = 'the fault/guilt'; che introduces a relative clause modifying colpa; là giù = 'down there'; cotanto costa = 'costs so much' meaning 'is punished so severely' (costa used figuratively of the punishment's severity).
segments.inferno-29-g24: Allor = 'then' (temporal adverb). Disse is the perfect of dire 'said'; 'l maestro = 'the master' (the article l' contracted before maestro). This is straightforward narrative attribution with inversion (Allor + verb + subject).
segments.inferno-29-g25: Non si franga is a negative command in an impersonal/reflexive/passive construction: si + present subjunctive franga (from frangere) = 'let not ... be broken' or 'do not let ... be diverted.' Lo tuo pensier = 'your thought' (lo = article, tuo = your, pensier = thought). Da qui innanzi = 'from now on / henceforth.' Sovr’ ello is sovra + ello = 'about it / concerning it' (ello = it); the apostrophe shows elision. Overall: 'Do not let your thought be turned away from it from now on.'
segments.inferno-29-g119: Simple narrative clause: 'E' is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this speech to the prior narration; 'io' is the explicit subject 'I' (emphatic here); 'dissi' is the first person singular passato remoto (literary past) of 'dire' meaning 'I said'; 'al poeta' is the indirect object formed by the preposition a + definite article (al = a + il) meaning 'to the poet'.
segments.inferno-29-g120: 'Or fu già mai gente sì vana come la sanese?' is a rhetorical question. 'Or' is a particle here introducing the question ('at this point/ever'); 'fu' is the passato remoto of 'essere' ('was' / 'has there been'); 'già mai' together function to mean 'ever' or 'at any time' (a traditional rhetorical combination); 'gente' is the noun 'people'; 'sì' is the adverb 'so' (intensifier); 'vana' is the feminine adjective 'vain' agreeing with 'gente'; 'come' = 'as/like'; 'la sanese' is a demonymal use with the article meaning 'the Sienese (people)'. The word order is typical for a rhetorical question in Dante: particle + past-tense verb + temporal adverb + noun phrase + predicative adjective + comparison.
segments.inferno-29-g121: Elliptical reply: 'Certo' = 'certainly' (emphatic particle); 'non' negates the following expression; 'la francesca' uses the article + proper name (here a familiar or local reference) — the article with a name is idiomatic in medieval Italian; 'sì d’assai' is an emphatic adverbial phrase: 'sì' = 'so', 'd’' = contraction of 'di' used adverbially, and 'assai' = 'much' or 'to a great degree'. Combined it expresses that Francesca is not so (to that degree).
segments.inferno-29-g122: Punctuation: a period (here closing the quoted speech or the short interjection).
segments.inferno-29-g123: 'Onde' here is a discourse particle meaning 'whereupon' or 'so/therefore' introducing the reply; 'l’altro' = 'the other' (masculine singular) and 'lebbroso' is a noun used as an epithet 'leper' (the sinner is described by his disease). The phrase sets up the speaker: 'the other leper.'
segments.inferno-29-g124: 'che m’intese' is a relative clause modifying 'l’altro lebbroso'. 'che' = 'who'; 'm’' is the clitic object pronoun 'mi' (me) contracted before a vowel; 'intese' is the passato remoto (third person singular) of 'intendere' meaning 'he heard/understood (me)'. The sequence literally 'who understood me.'
segments.inferno-29-g125: 'rispuose al detto mio:' is the main reporting clause. 'rispuose' is passato remoto of 'rispondere' ('he replied'); 'al' = a + il 'to the'; 'detto' = 'saying/remark' (a noun here); 'mio' = 'my' — 'he replied to my remark.'
segments.inferno-29-g126: Quoted enumeration begins. 'Tra’mene' is an archaic contraction: here it functions like 'tra me ne' / 'tra di loro' — 'among them (I name)'; the clause then gives a name: 'Stricca' (proper name). 'che' = 'who'; 'seppe' is passato remoto of 'sapere' used with the sense 'knew how / could' (so best rendered 'knew how to'); 'far le temperate spese' = 'to make (or meet) the temperate/moderate expenses' — 'far' is the infinitive 'to make/do' and 'le temperate spese' is the object phrase.
segments.inferno-29-g127: Continuation of the list: 'e Niccolò' = 'and Niccolò'; 'che' = 'who'; 'la costuma ricca' = 'the (plant's or thing's) rich habit' (here 'costuma/costuma' denotes habit/characteristic); 'del garofano' = 'of the garofano' (the plant); 'prima discoverse' = 'first discovered/uncovered' ('prima' = 'first' or 'previously', 'discoverse' = passato remoto 'discovered'); 'ne l’orto' = 'in the garden' (ne = in; l'orto = the garden); 'dove tal seme s’appicca' = 'where such a seed takes root' — 's’ ' is the reflexive clitic (si) and 'appicca' = 'attaches/takes root'.
segments.inferno-29-g128: More of the catalogue: 'e tra’ne la brigata' = 'and among them the band/company' (again 'tra’ne' = 'among them'); 'in che disperse Caccia d’Ascian' = 'in which Caccia of Asciano scattered/dispersed' — 'disperse' is here a past or narrative infinitive/participle form meaning 'scattered'; 'la vigna e la gran fonda' = 'the vineyard and the great fund/store' (articles + nouns). Word order places the relative/incident clause after the noun as in Italian.
segments.inferno-29-g129: Closing the enumeration: 'e l’Abbagliato suo senno proferse.' 'e' = 'and'; 'l’Abbagliato' is a nickname or epithet 'the Dazzled (one)'; 'suo' = 'his' (referring to that person's); 'senno' = 'sense/mind/opinion'; 'proferse' is passato remoto of 'proferire' meaning 'he uttered/expressed'. So 'and the one called the Dazzled expressed his opinion.'
segments.inferno-29-g44: Quand o + passato remoto: 'Quando noi fummo' is a simple temporal clause using the passato remoto 'fummo' (we were). 'sor' is a poetic contraction of 'sopra' meaning 'upon' or 'on', and it governs the following article + noun 'l'ultima chiostra' (the last cloister). 'di Malebolge' is a normal prepositional phrase identifying the place.
segments.inferno-29-g45: 'sì che' introduces a result clause: 'so that'. 'potean' is the archaic/poetic imperfect 'potevano' (they could) and is followed by the infinitive 'parere' (to appear/seem). 'A la veduta nostra' is an indirect complement ('to our view' = 'from our vantage point'). 'I suoi conversi' is the subject (literally 'its conversi'—the dwellers/converted of that place).
segments.inferno-29-g46: Syntactically the plural noun 'lamenti' is the subject and 'saettaron' is the passato remoto 3rd‑person plural of a transitive verb meaning 'they shot' (literally 'they shot like arrows'). The object pronoun 'me' follows the verb here (a poetic ordering), and 'diversi' is an adjective meaning 'various' that in the verse is placed after the object pronoun but logically modifies 'lamenti'.
segments.inferno-29-g47: This is a relative/explanatory clause ('che ... avean li strali') modifying the preceding 'lamenti'. 'avean' is the imperfect 'had' (3rd pl.), and 'li strali' is the subject of that verb. 'di pietà ferrati' is a prepositional phrase with a past participle used attributively: 'ferrati' (iron‑shod/metal‑edged) modified by 'di pietà' ('with pity' or 'by pity'), a poetic image applied to the arrows.
segments.inferno-29-g48: 'Ond'' (onde) here introduces consequence ('wherefore' / 'therefore'). The pronoun 'io' is the subject and 'li orecchi' (the ears) is the direct object; 'con le man' is an instrumental phrase ('with my hands'), and 'copersi' is the passato remoto 1st‑person singular of 'coprire' ('I covered'). Note the poetic word order: object before verb.
segments.inferno-29-g49: 'Qual dolor fora' is a rhetorical conditional main clause: 'What pain would there be'. 'Qual' = 'what/such', 'dolor' = 'pain/sorrow', and 'fora' is the conditional form (archaic) of 'essere' ('would be').
segments.inferno-29-g50: This long subordinate 'if' clause begins with 'se' (if) and lists origins of disease: 'de li spedali di Valdichiana' (from the hospitals of Valdichiana), with a time specification 'tra ’l luglio e ’l settembre' (between July and September), and adds 'e di Maremma e di Sardigna' (and of the Maremma and Sardinia). The verb 'fossero' is the imperfect subjunctive/conditional subordinate ('were') that completes the conditional construction; 'in una fossa tutti ’nsembre' = 'all together in one pit.'
segments.inferno-29-g51: 'Tal era quivi' is an inversion meaning 'such was there' = 'such (a thing) existed there' or more naturally 'such was it there.' 'Tal' = 'such', 'era' = imperfect 'was', 'quivi' = 'there' (poetic).
segments.inferno-29-g52: This sentence continues with 'e tal puzzo n’usciva' — 'and such a stench issued from it.' The clitic 'n’' is 'ne' (from it/of it). 'qual suol venir' is a comparative relative clause: 'as is wont to come' ('suol' = habitual 'solere' + infinitive 'venir'). 'de le marcite membre' = 'from the rotten limbs.'
segments.inferno-29-g53: 'Noi discendemmo' is passato remoto 'we descended'. The phrase 'in su' (in + su) is a locative 'onto' or 'up on', and 'l'ultima riva del lungo scoglio' is the noun phrase 'the last bank/shore of the long rock/reef.'
segments.inferno-29-g54: 'pur' is an adverb here meaning 'still' / 'even'; 'da man sinistra' is an idiomatic locative phrase meaning 'on the left hand/side' (literally 'from the left hand'). The construction indicates direction/position kept while descending.
segments.inferno-29-g55: 'E allor fu la mia vista più viva' = 'and then my sight was more lively/keen' ('fu' = past historic of 'to be'). 'giù ver' lo fondo' is an idiomatic locution 'down toward the bottom' ('giù' = down, 'ver'' = contraction of 'verso' = toward). Word order places the directional phrase after the verb phrase.
segments.inferno-29-g56: 'la 've' is a fused locative ('la' + 've' = 'where'); the phrase names an agent in apposition: 'la ministra de l'alto Sire infallibil giustizia' = 'the minister/agent of the High Lord — infallible Justice.' 'Punisce' is present indicative 'punishes', and the relative clause 'che qui registra' ('which/whom it records here') modifies 'i falsador' (the falsifiers).
segments.inferno-29-g57: 'Non credo' = 'I do not believe'. The construction 'ch’a veder' ('that to see') is idiomatic: 'that, to see, ...' and introduces what follows; 'fosse' is the imperfect subjunctive (or conditional subordinate) of 'essere' expressing the hypothetical/comparative 'there would be' or 'there was'. 'Il popol tutto infermo' is the nominal predicate 'the whole people sick.'
segments.inferno-29-g58: 'quando fu l’aere sì pien di malizia' is a temporal clause: 'when the air was so full of malice/pestilence.' 'fu' = past historic 'was'; 'sì pien' = 'so full'. Note that 'malizia' in context refers to pestilential disease rather than moral malice (a semantic nuance).
segments.inferno-29-g59: 'che' introduces a result/cause clause: 'that' / 'such that'. 'li animali' is the subject 'the animals.' The clause that follows explains the effect on animals.
segments.inferno-29-g60: 'infino al picciol vermo' = 'even down to the smallest worm' (infino = 'up to/even to', 'picciol' = archaic form of 'piccolo'). This phrase emphasizes that even the tiniest creatures were affected.
segments.inferno-29-g61: 'cascaron' is the passato remoto 3rd plural of 'cascar' (to fall), 'cascaron tutti' = 'they all fell' (i.e., perished or collapsed). The verb and following 'tutti' make the universal effect explicit.
segments.inferno-29-g62: 'e poi le genti antiche' = 'and afterwards the ancient peoples' — 'poi' = then/afterwards, 'le genti antiche' = the ancient peoples (pl.). This announces a later effect on human populations.
segments.inferno-29-g63: 'secondo che i poeti hanno per fermo' = 'according to what the poets hold as certain.' 'Secondo che' = 'according to (that)'; 'hanno per fermo' is an idiom: 'take as fixed/certain.'
segments.inferno-29-g64: 'si ristorar di seme di formiche' means 'they were restored/replenished by the seed/seedlings of ants.' 'Si' is reflexive/impersonal (they/themself), 'ristorar' = 'to restore/refresh', and the construction 'di seme di formiche' indicates the source/means (seed of ants).
segments.inferno-29-g65: The final clause 'ch’era a veder per quella oscura valle languir li spirti per diverse biche' = 'for it was to be seen through that dark valley the spirits languishing in various heaps.' 'Ch’era a veder' is an idiom meaning 'for it was to be seen', 'languir' is infinitive 'to languish', 'li spirti' = the spirits/souls (here meaning the souls of the punished), and 'per diverse biche' indicates they lay in several heaps/stacks.
segments.inferno-29-g66: Qual ... e qual ... is a distributive construction meaning “some ... and some ...”. Sovra 'l ventre / sovra le spalle are prepositional phrases fronted for emphasis (‘over/on the belly’; ‘over/on the shoulders’). L’un de l’altro is a reciprocal expression lit. “one of the other” (= one on top of the other). Giacea is the imperfect indicative (3rd singular) of giacere, used generically here to say that (each) one lay (i.e. it’s as if ‘one lay’ is repeated for each instance).
segments.inferno-29-g67: The e continues the catalogue (‘and some …’). Carpone is an adjectival/adverbial term meaning ‘on all fours’ or ‘crawling’. Si trasmutava is the imperfect reflexive form of trasmutare (3rd singular) meaning ‘(one) was transformed/changing (into that state)’; the si marks the change as reflexive/inchoative (‘change oneself / become’). Per lo tristo calle = ‘along/through the wretched path’, with per indicating movement/along.
segments.inferno-29-g68: Passo passo is a repetitive adverbial phrase meaning ‘step by step’ or ‘pace by pace’. Andavam is the imperfect (3rd pl.) of andare (here poetic/archaic andavam for modern andavano), signalling ongoing movement. Sanza is the archaic form of senza (‘without’); sermone = ‘speech/discourse’, so sanza sermone = ‘without speaking’. The adverbial phrase precedes the verb for emphasis/metrical effect.
segments.inferno-29-g69: Guardando e ascoltando are gerunds (present participles) that describe simultaneous actions accompanying andavam: ‘watching and listening’. Li ammalati is the direct object: li = the (archaic plural), ammalati = ‘the sick’ (past participle used substantivally). The two gerunds coordinate to show what they did while going.
segments.inferno-29-g70: Che introduces a relative clause modifying li ammalati: ‘who/which’. Non potean is the imperfect (3rd pl.) of potere in archaic meter form (potean = potevano), expressing inability. Levar is an infinitive meaning ‘to raise/lift’ (here: lift their bodies). Le lor persone = ‘their persons/bodies’ (le = feminine plural article; lor = their). The structure is a relative clause describing the sick.
segments.inferno-29-g71: Io vidi = simple past (‘I saw’). Due sedere a sé poggiati: sedere here is a verbal noun/infinitive idea ('to sit' / 'seated'), and poggiati is a past participle agreeing with due (plural) meaning ‘leaning/leaned’. A sé is reciprocal/reflexive (‘against each other’ / ‘back to back’). The clause means ‘I saw two seated, leaning against one another.’
segments.inferno-29-g72: Com’ a scaldar = ‘as (when) for warming/ to warm’; si poggia is a present (3rd sg.) impersonal/reciprocal ‘one rests / they rest (against one another)’. Tegghia a tegghia is a repetitive, tile‑by‑tile image (tegghia = pan or tile), describing how they lean like one tile on another to warm. The present tense in the simile gives a timeless picture.
segments.inferno-29-g73: Dal capo al piè = idiom ‘from head to foot’ (dal = da + il; al = a + il). Di schianze macolati: di is partitive/'with', schianze (here) refers to scabby/patchy places or crusts on the skin, and macolati is a past participle used adjectivally (‘stained/spotted’). The overall sense: ‘stained from head to foot with scabby patches.’
segments.inferno-29-g74: E non vidi già mai menare stregghia = ‘and I had never yet seen [anyone] use/brandish a currycomb’. Menare is an infinitive used as object of vidi (‘see [someone] do X’). Già mai together with non gives the sense ‘never before’. A ragazzo aspettato dal segnorso = ‘on a boy awaited by/expected by his master’ (aspettato used as past participle/adjectival).
segments.inferno-29-g75: Né = ‘nor / not even’. A colui che mal volontier vegghia = ‘to the one who is unwillingly watchful’ or ‘to him who would rather not keep watch’. Mal volontier is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘reluctantly / unwillingly’. Vegghia (from vegliare) = ‘keep watch / stay awake’. The clause continues the negative comparison (I never saw … nor to …).
segments.inferno-29-g76: Come ciascun menava spesso il morso de l’unghie sopra sé = ‘for each one often bore/carraied the bite of the nail upon himself’: come = ‘as/for’, ciascun = ‘each one’, menava is imperfect ‘used to carry/was carrying’, il morso de l’unghie = ‘the bite of the nail’ (they were biting or carrying marks of nail‑bites), sopra sé = ‘upon himself’. Per la gran rabbia del pizzicor = ‘because of the great agony/angry torment of the itching’ (per = because of).
segments.inferno-29-g77: Che introduces a relative clause referring to the pizzicor: ‘which has no more help/remedy’. Non ha più soccorso: ha is present (3rd sg.) of avere; più = any longer; soccorso = ‘aid/relief’. The present highlights the (ongoing) absence of remedy.
segments.inferno-29-g78: E sì traevan giù l’unghie la scabbia = ‘and so with their nails they pulled the scab down’. Traevan is the imperfect (3rd pl.) of trarre (here: ‘draw/pull off’). Giù = down; word order is inverted: the agentive instrument (le unghie) and the direct object (la scabbia) are ordered for rhythm — meaning they scraped/pulled the scab off with their nails.
segments.inferno-29-g79: Come introduces a simile: ‘as a knife (coltel) of the rudd strips off the scales’. Coltel di scardova = ‘knife of the rudd’ (scardova = rudd, a fish). Le scaglie = the scales (direct object). O d’altro pesce che più larghe l’abbia = ‘or of some other fish that has them larger’ — che + abbia uses the subjunctive (abbia) because the antecedent is indefinite/comparative; l’ is the clitic pronoun referring back to le scaglie (‘has them’).
segments.inferno-29-g80: O tu = vocative ‘O you’. Che introduces a descriptive relative clause ‘who…’. Con le dita = instrument ‘with your fingers’. Ti dismaglie is reflexive: dismagliare (dismagliere) with the clitic ti means ‘you unmesh / you tear open (the mail) with your fingers’ — present indicative 2nd sg. The reflexive/clitic placement is normal; the clause addresses the person’s action.
segments.inferno-29-g81: Cominciò is the passato remoto (3rd sg.) ‘he began’. ’L duca mio = ‘my duke/guide’ (article + noun + possessive). A l’un di loro = indirect object ‘to one of them’ (a = to; l’un di loro = one of them). The verb cominciò governs the direct speech that follows; word order places the speaker before the indirect object.
segments.inferno-29-g82: E che fai d’esse talvolta tanaglie = ‘and what do you do of / about being sometimes pincers’. Fai is 2nd singular present of fare (‘do’). D’esse is the contracted di + essere (‘to be’), so the phrase asks about the fact that he sometimes acts as tanaglie (pincers). Talvolta = ‘sometimes’; tanaglie = ‘pincers’ used metaphorically.
segments.inferno-29-g83: Dinne is an imperative form (dirne) meaning ‘tell (me) of it / tell (me)’. S’alcun is a contraction of se alcun = ‘if any’ / ‘whether any’. Latino = ‘a Latin’ (a man of Latin speech / Latin people). È is present of essere ‘is’. Tra costoro = ‘among these people’. Che son quinc’ entro = relative clause ‘who are in here’ (son = archaic plural of sono; quinc’ = qui + enclitic; entro = within). Overall: ‘tell me whether any Latin is among those in here.’
segments.inferno-29-g84: Se l’unghia ti basti etternalmente a cotesto lavoro = indirect question ‘if the nail will suffice you forever for this work’. Se = ‘if/whether’ introducing the question. L’unghia (the nail) is the grammatical subject; ti is the dative clitic ‘to you’; basti is present subjunctive (3rd sg.) of bastare used because this is an indirect question/uncertainty. Etternalmente = ‘eternally / forever’; cotesto lavoro = ‘this task’ (cotesto = that/this near you).
segments.inferno-29-g26: Attendi is the 2nd-person singular imperative of attendere meaning 'attend to' or 'pay attention to'. Ad altro is the prepositional phrase 'to/at something else' (ad + altro), with altro functioning as an indefinite pronoun/adverb meaning 'else' or 'something else'.
segments.inferno-29-g27: Ed is the conjunction 'and'. Ei is the subject pronoun 'he' (archaic form). Là is an adverb 'there'. Si rimanga is composed of the reflexive particle si plus rimanga, the present subjunctive (3rd sg) of rimanere used here in a jussive/optative sense ('let him remain' / 'may he remain'); older Italian uses the subjunctive to issue such commands.
segments.inferno-29-g28: Ch’io vidi = 'that I saw' (ch’ = che 'that'; vidi = passato remoto of vedere, 'I saw'). Lui a piè del ponticello is an adverbial phrase 'him at the foot of the little bridge' (a + piè = 'at the foot of'; del = di + il). Mostrarti e minacciar are infinitives functioning as the complements of vidi: 'I saw him (mostrarti) and (minacciar)' — mostrarti is mostrare + enclitic ti 'to/at you' (point out/show you), minacciar is the truncated infinitive of minacciare 'to threaten'. Forte is used adverbially 'forcefully/loudly'. Col = con + il 'with the'; dito = 'finger'.
segments.inferno-29-g29: E = 'and'. Udi’ is the past tense 'I heard' (udire) with the terminal apostrophe marking elision. Nominar is the infinitive 'to name/mention' used as the complement of udi’. Geri del Bello is a proper name; del + Bello here forms the surname 'del Bello'.
segments.inferno-29-g30: Tu eri = 'you were' (eri, imperfect or past copula). Allor = 'then'. Sì del tutto = an adverbial cluster 'so/so completely' (sì 'so' + del tutto 'entirely'). Impedito is a past participle used adjectivally meaning 'hindered' or 'preoccupied'. Sovra here means 'about' or 'concerning' and introduces the person referred to by colui che già tenne Altaforte (colui = 'that one/he who'; già tenne = passato remoto 'held' or 'occupied', i.e. 'who once held/held sway over Altaforte').
segments.inferno-29-g31: Che is the relative conjunction 'that'/'who' introducing the result clause. Non is the negation 'not'. Guardasti is the passato remoto 2nd singular of guardare 'you looked' (here 'you looked/brought your gaze'); in là = 'beyond'/'further' (in + là). The whole clause means 'that you did not look further'.
segments.inferno-29-g32: Sì is an adverb 'thus/so' introducing the consequence. Fu partito is a compound of fu (passato remoto of essere) + partito (past participle of partire) meaning 'he departed' or 'he was gone' — a simple past narrative statement ('so he departed').
segments.inferno-29-g33: O is the vocative interjection 'O' (oh). Duca mio is the vocative 'my leader/duke' (duca = guide/duke; mio 'my'), a direct address to Virgil.
segments.inferno-29-g34: La vïolenta morte = 'the violent death' (la + morte, modified by vïolenta). Che non li è vendicata ancor is a relative clause: che = 'which', non = 'not', li is an older clitic standing for 'to him' / 'for him' (here marking the person affected), è = 'is', vendicata = past participle 'avenged' used in a stative/passive sense, ancor = 'yet/still'. So: 'the violent death which has not yet been avenged for him'.
segments.inferno-29-g35: Diss’ io is the inverted narrative past 'I said' (diss' = dissi, passato remoto of dire; io = 'I'), marking the speaker's utterance.
segments.inferno-29-g36: Per alcun che = 'because of someone who' or 'for some person who' (per = 'for/on account of'; alcun = 'someone/anyone' here 'some person'; che = 'who'). De l’onta = 'of the dishonor' (de = 'of/from'; l’onta = 'the shame/dishonor' — the lemma list here is imperfect but context yields 'shame'). Sia consorte uses the present subjunctive sia (from essere) + consorte 'a companion/partner' meaning 'is partner/associated with' (i.e. 'is implicated in the dishonor').
segments.inferno-29-g37: Fece lui disdegnoso = 'this made him disdainful' (fece = past of fare 'made/caused'; lui = 'him'; disdegnoso = adjective 'disdainful/haughty'). The structure is causal: because of that (the unavenged death) he became disdainful.
segments.inferno-29-g38: Ond’ = 'wherefore/therefore' (ond' = da cui/for which reason). Sen gio is the contracted archaic phrase s'en giò meaning 'he went down/away' (sen = se + ne 'of it/from it/away', gio = giò = 'down'). Sanza parlarmi = 'without speaking to me' (sanza = senza 'without'; parlarmi = parlare + mi enclitic 'to speak to me').
segments.inferno-29-g39: Sì com’ ïo estimo = 'as I judge/think' (sì = 'so/thus', com' = come 'as', ïo = 'I', estimo = 1st person singular 'I esteem/judge' used to mean 'I think').
segments.inferno-29-g40: E in ciò m’ha el fatto a sé più pio = 'and in that (respect) he has made me more compassionate toward him.' E = 'and'; in ciò = 'in that / because of that'; m' = mi 'me' as direct object; ha = present perfect auxiliary 'has'; el = subject clitic 'he'; fatto = past participle 'made' (ha fatto = 'has made'); a sé = 'to himself/toward him'; più pio = 'more compassionate/pious'. The phrase means the restraint (his not speaking) has made the speaker feel more pity/affection for him.
segments.inferno-29-g41: Così parlammo = 'Thus we spoke' (parlammo, passato remoto 1st pl of parlare). Infino al loco primo = 'as far as the first place' (infino a + il). Che de lo scoglio l’altra valle mostra is a relative clause modifying loco primo: che = 'where/that', de lo scoglio = 'from the rock/cliff', l’altra valle = 'the other valley', mostra = present indicative 'shows/appears' (poetic present used in narrative description).
segments.inferno-29-g42: Se più lume vi fosse = 'if there were more light there' (se = 'if'; più lume = 'more light'; vi = locative 'there'; fosse = imperfect/subjunctive of essere 'were', marking a hypothetical).
segments.inferno-29-g43: Tutto ad imo = an adverbial phrase 'all down to the bottom' (tutto = 'all/entirely', ad = 'to', imo = 'the lowest/deepest part' = 'the bottom'). It completes the conditional idea: were there more light, one could see it all the way to the bottom.
segments.inferno-29-g95: Lo buon maestro is a normal article + adjective + noun order; Lo is the archaic article used before an adjective. a me marks the indirect object (“to me”), and tutto functions adverbially with the reflexive verb s’accolse (s’ = si): tutto s’accolse = “he received/embraced me completely / drew entirely close to me.” Accolse is passato remoto of accogliere.
segments.inferno-29-g96: Dicendo is a gerund (from dire) that links the action of the main clause to reported speech; it means “saying” and introduces the direct quotation.
segments.inferno-29-g97: Dì is an imperative (‘tell’), followed by the preposition a + indirect-object pronoun lor (“to them”). ciò che tu vuoli is an indirect question/relative clause meaning ‘whatsoever you want’; vuoli is the archaic/poetic second-person form of volere (‘you want’).
segments.inferno-29-g98: e io incominciai = ‘and I began’; incominciai is passato remoto (I began), with io as the subject. The conjunction e simply links the sentence to the previous speech act.
segments.inferno-29-g99: poscia ch’ei volse = ‘after he turned.’ Poscia = ‘afterwards/after’; ch’ = che (that/after); ei is the archaic form of egli (he); volse is passato remoto of volgere, here meaning ‘turned (his head/toward me).’
segments.inferno-29-g100: Se introduces a conditional (‘if’). la vostra memoria = ‘your memory/reputation’; non s’imboli is a reflexive present-subjunctive construction (s’ = si) of imbolire (here “fade/vanish”), meaning ‘has not vanished.’ nel primo mondo da l’umane menti = ‘in the first world from human minds,’ where primo mondo means the earlier/first life or the earliest sphere of human remembrance.
segments.inferno-29-g101: ma s’ella viva = ‘but if it/she lives’; here s’ is a contraction of se or si in old usage and viva is present subjunctive (or indicative in sense) of vivere used in the conditional sense begun by Se. sotto molti soli = literally ‘under many suns,’ a poetic phrase meaning surviving many ages or many cycles.
segments.inferno-29-g102: ditemi is 2nd-person plural imperative + enclitic me: ‘tell me.’ chi voi siete = ‘who you are’ (voi + siete = present copula). e di che genti = ‘and of what people’ (di che = ‘of what’ introducing the noun genti ‘people/nation’).
segments.inferno-29-g103: la vostra sconcia e fastidiosa pena = ‘your shameful and wearisome punishment/suffering.’ di palesarvi a me is an infinitival phrase ‘to reveal yourselves to me’ (palesarvi = palesare + enclitic vi). non vi spaventi is a jussive/negative exhortation using the present subjunctive spaventi (‘may … not frighten’ or ‘do not let … frighten you’), with vi as the clitic object ‘you.’
segments.inferno-29-g104: Io fui d’Arezzo = ‘I was from Arezzo.’ Fui is passato remoto of essere (‘I was’). d’ is the contracted preposition di meaning ‘from’ before the place name.
segments.inferno-29-g105: e Albero da Siena is a continuation identifying the speaker as ‘Albero (of) Siena’; here Albero is a proper name (not the common noun albero ‘tree’). da marks origin (‘from Siena’).
segments.inferno-29-g106: rispuose l’un = ‘one replied.’ Rispuose is passato remoto of rispondere (‘replied’). l’un (short for l’uno) means ‘the one’ or ‘one of them.’
segments.inferno-29-g107: mi fé mettere al foco = causative construction: fé (past tense of fare, ‘made/caused’) + infinitive mettere = ‘had me put to the fire’ (i.e., they burned me). mi is the clitic object ‘me’; al foco = ‘to the fire’ (foco = archaic foco/fuoco).
segments.inferno-29-g108: ma quel per ch’io mori’ qui non mi mena = ‘but the one by whom I died here does not lead me.’ ma = ‘but’; quel = ‘that one/the man’; per ch’io mori’ is a relative clause ‘by/through whom I died’ (mori’ = morì, passato remoto of morire); non mi mena = ‘does not lead me’ (mena = menare, present).
segments.inferno-29-g109: Vero è ch’i’ dissi lui = ‘It is true that I said to him’ (Vero è che is an emphatic formula). Dis­si is passato remoto of dire (‘I said’); lui is the indirect object/recipient.
segments.inferno-29-g110: parlando a gioco = a gerundial phrase ‘speaking as a joke’ or ‘in jest’; parlando (gerund of parlare) qualifies the manner of the saying, and a gioco is an idiom meaning ‘in jest.’
segments.inferno-29-g111: I’ mi saprei levar per l’aere a volo = ‘I could lift myself into the air in flight.’ Saprei is the conditional of sapere used here to express capacity/willingness (‘I could’); levar (levare) is the infinitive ‘to raise/rise,’ and per l’aere a volo gives the path/manner ‘through the air, in flight.’
segments.inferno-29-g112: e quei = ‘and he’ (quei is an archaic/dislocated form meaning ‘that one’ or ‘he’); it resumes the man who spoke. The conjunction links the narrator’s remark to the addressee’s reaction.
segments.inferno-29-g113: ch’avea vaghezza e senno poco = ‘who had desire/longing and little sense.’ Avea is the imperfect (‘he had’); vaghezza = inclination/longing; senno poco = ‘little judgment’ (poco postpositive modifies senno).
segments.inferno-29-g114: volle ch’i’ li mostrassi l’arte = ‘he wanted that I show him the skill/art.’ Volle is passato remoto of volere (‘he wanted’); the subordinate clause uses the imperfect/subjunctive mostrassi (subjunctive in reported desire) with enclitic li = ‘to him’; l’arte = ‘the art/skill.’
segments.inferno-29-g115: e solo perch’ io nol feci Dedalo = ‘and only because I did not do it [for] Dedalus.’ Solo = ‘only’; perch’ = perché ‘because’; nol = non lo (did not do it); feci is passato remoto of fare (‘I did/made’); Dedalo here is the proper name Daedalus.
segments.inferno-29-g116: mi fece ardere a tal che l’avea per figliuolo = causative construction mi fece + infinitive ardere = ‘he made me burn’ (caused me to be burned). a tal che l’avea per figliuolo is a relative/result clause ‘by such a one whom he had as a son’ (avea per figliuolo = ‘he held as a son’).
segments.inferno-29-g117: Ma ne l’ultima bolgia de le diece = ‘But in the last ditch/pit of the ten.’ Ne = in/within; l’ultima bolgia = ‘the last bolgia’ (one of the ten Malebolge ditches); de le diece = ‘of the ten.’ me per l’alchìmia che nel mondo usai = ‘me, because of the alchemy that I used in the world’: per = ‘because of/for’; usai is passato remoto of usare (‘I used’). dannò Minòs = ‘Minos condemned.’ Dannò is passato remoto.
segments.inferno-29-g118: a cui fallar non lece = relative clause modifying Minòs: a cui = ‘to whom’; fallar (infinitive) = ‘to fail/escape/deceive’ in context meaning ‘to deceive/escape him’; non lece = ‘is not permitted’ (lece is archaic for liece/lice, ‘it is permitted’). The whole line means ‘to whom it is not permitted to be deceived/for anyone to escape.’

sentences.inferno-29-s1: So many people and the various wounds had so intoxicated my eyes that they longed for nothing but to remain weeping.
sentences.inferno-29-s2: But Virgil said to me: "What are you still watching?"
sentences.inferno-29-s3: Why is your sight still overcome down there among the sad, truncated shades?
sentences.inferno-29-s4: You did not behave so in the other bolges; think, if you mean to count them, that the valley winds for twenty-two miles.
sentences.inferno-29-s21: "We are Latins, both of us whom you see so ruined here," one answered, weeping; "but who are you who asked about us?"
sentences.inferno-29-s23: And the duke said: "I am one who descends with this living man, down from leap to leap, and I intend to show him Hell."
sentences.inferno-29-s24: Then the common support broke; and, trembling, each one turned to me with others who had heard it by the rebound.
sentences.inferno-29-s33: But so that you may know who thus backs you against the Sienese, sharpen your eye toward me, so that my face may clearly answer you: thus you will see that I am the shade of Capocchio, who counterfeited the coinage by means of alchemy; and you must remember, if I well behold you, how by nature I was a good ape.
sentences.inferno-29-s5: And already the moon is beneath our feet; there is little time now that has been allotted to us, and there are other things to see that you do not see.
sentences.inferno-29-s6: "If you had," I answered shortly after, "paid attention to the reason for which I was watching, perhaps you would still have kept me in that downcast state."
sentences.inferno-29-s7: Some of them went on, and I walked behind him; the master, already making the answer and adding, said: "Inside that hollow where I now kept my eyes so fixed, I believe that a spirit of my blood laments the guilt that down there is so dearly punished."
sentences.inferno-29-s8: Then the master said: "Do not let your thought be broken away from it henceforth."
sentences.inferno-29-s29: And I said to the poet: "Was there ever a people so vain as the Sienese?"
sentences.inferno-29-s32: Whereupon the other leper, who had understood me, replied to my remark: "Among them — Stricca, who knew how to make moderate expenditures; and Niccolò, who first uncovered the garofano's rich habit in the garden where such a seed takes root; and among them the band in which Caccia of Asciano scattered the vineyard and the great fund; and the one called 'the Dazzled' voiced his mind."
sentences.inferno-29-s13: When we were upon the last cloister of Malebolge, so that its inhabitants could be seen from our vantage point, various lamentations shot at me, for their arrows were iron‑edged with pity; wherefore I covered my ears with my hands.
sentences.inferno-29-s14: What pain it would be, if the ills from the hospitals of the Chiana Valley between July and September and those of the Maremma and Sardinia were all together in one pit — such was it there, and such a stench came from it as is wont to come from rotten limbs.
sentences.inferno-29-s15: We went down onto the far bank of the long rock, keeping to the left hand; and then my sight became keener down toward the bottom, where the minister of the Most High Lord — infallible Justice — punishes the falsifiers that are recorded here.
sentences.inferno-29-s16: I do not believe that the whole people of Aegina were ever so completely stricken with sickness at the sight of greater misery, when the air was so full of pestilence that all the animals, down to the smallest worm, fell; and afterwards the ancient peoples, according to what the poets firmly hold, were restored by the seed of ants; for it was to be seen through that dark valley the spirits languishing in various heaps.
sentences.inferno-29-s17: Some lay with one on another's belly and some on each other's shoulders, and some crawled on all fours, changing themselves along the wretched path.
sentences.inferno-29-s18: Step by step they went without speech, watching and listening to the sick, who could not raise their bodies.
sentences.inferno-29-s19: I saw two seated leaning against each other, like tiles set tile to tile to warm one another, stained from head to foot with scabby patches; and I never saw anyone use a currycomb on a boy waiting for his master, nor on the man who reluctantly keeps watch, for each one would often carry the bite of his nail upon himself because of the great torment of the itch — which has no remedy; and so with their nails they pulled the scab down, like a knife strips the scales from the rudd or from some other fish that has them larger.
sentences.inferno-29-s20: “O you who unmesh (it) with your fingers,” my duke began to one of them, “and how is it that you sometimes act as pincers? Tell me whether any Latin is among these who are in here, and whether the nail will be enough for you forever for this task.”
sentences.inferno-29-s9: Pay attention to something else, and let him remain there; for I saw him at the foot of the little bridge pointing you out and threatening strongly with his finger, and I heard him name 'Geri del Bello'.
sentences.inferno-29-s10: You were then so entirely preoccupied with the man who once held Hautefort that you did not look beyond, and so he had departed.
sentences.inferno-29-s11: "O my leader, I said, 'the violent death which has not yet been avenged for him, because someone is partner in the dishonor, made him disdainful; wherefore he went down without speaking to me, as I think; and in that he has made me more sympathetic toward him.'"
sentences.inferno-29-s12: Thus we spoke as far as the first place where, from the crag, the other valley appears — if there had been more light there, all the way to the bottom.
sentences.inferno-29-s25: My good master drew entirely close to me, saying, “Tell them whatever you wish”; and I began, after he turned, “If your memory has not vanished from the first world of human minds, but if it lives under many suns, tell me who you are and of what people; let not your shameful and wearisome punishment frighten you from revealing yourselves to me.”
sentences.inferno-29-s26: “I was from Arezzo, Albero from Siena,” one answered; “they had me put to the fire; but the one by whom I died here does not lead me.”
sentences.inferno-29-s27: It is true that I told him, speaking in jest: “I could lift myself up through the air in flight”; and he, who had longing and little sense, wanted that I show him the art; and only because I did not do it [for] Dedalus he caused me to be burned by the very man whom he considered his son.
sentences.inferno-29-s28: But in the last pit of the ten he condemned me, for the alchemy that I used in the world, to Minos, to whom it is not permitted to deceive (or escape).
